skill.name: azure-mgmt-apimanagement-dotnet
skill.description: Guidance for using the Azure Resource Manager API Management SDK in .NET to create and manage APIM services, APIs, products, subscriptions, policies, backups, and related resources.
skill.summary: Use Azure.ResourceManager.ApiManagement in .NET for Azure API Management management-plane tasks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, network, credential, weak-crypto, and dangerous-combination patterns, but most are false positives caused by Markdown fences, C# sample code, Azure documentation links, and APIM text. The file is documentation-only and shows no prompt injection or malicious exfiltration, but it includes an example that retrieves and prints an APIM subscription primary key, which is a real secret-handling risk.

medium_findings.title: Subscription Key Printed in Example
medium_findings.description: The sample retrieves APIM subscription secrets and writes the primary key to console output. This can expose credentials in logs, terminals, or assistant transcripts if copied into real automation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly calls GetSecretsAsync and prints keys.Value.PrimaryKey. This is a clear secret-disclosure anti-pattern, even though it appears in documentation rather than executable skill code.

medium_findings.title: Service Principal Secret Documented as Environment Variable
medium_findings.description: The documentation lists AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET as an optional authentication variable. This is normal for Azure SDK examples, but users should prefer managed identity or secret stores and avoid exposing this value to assistant context.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable is explicitly present and can contain a credential. The surrounding context is legitimate Azure authentication guidance, so this is a managed risk rather than malicious behavior.

low_findings.title: External Command Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown code fences or inline code formatting. The only actual shell commands are dotnet package installation examples, and no dynamic command execution is present in the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows Markdown fences, C# samples, tables, and inline identifiers rather than Ruby backticks. The shell content is static package installation guidance.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Placeholder Endpoints
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings point to a sample contoso backend endpoint and official Microsoft documentation links. No code sends local files, credentials, or environment variables to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible documentation examples and official reference links. I found no evidence of network exfiltration or hidden callbacks.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are String-Matching False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography findings do not identify a cryptographic algorithm. The cited lines contain frontmatter description text and product description text, not MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of both cited locations found plain text descriptions only. No cryptographic implementation or algorithm selection is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret Value Written to Console
dangerous_patterns.description: A C# example prints an APIM subscription primary key. Generated code from this skill should redact keys and avoid logging secret values.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The example explicitly writes the primary key to standard output. This is directly observable in the cited lines.

content.value_statement: Azure API Management management-plane work requires many related SDK resource types and long-running operations. This skill gives focused .NET guidance for APIM services, APIs, products, subscriptions, policies, and backups.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to install Azure.ResourceManager.ApiManagement and Azure.Identity packages.
Shows DefaultAzureCredential setup for Azure Resource Manager clients.
Provides examples for creating APIM services, APIs, products, and subscriptions.
Documents APIM policy creation using XML policy content.
Covers backup and restore calls using managed identity storage access.
Lists key APIM SDK resource types, SKU types, and reference topics.
limitations: Focuses on management-plane ARM operations, not APIM gateway data-plane calls.
Does not validate live Azure configuration, permissions, regions, or quotas.
Examples may need updates for newer SDK versions or tenant-specific policies.
Secret-handling examples require review before use in production automation.

use_cases.title: Provision a Test APIM Service
use_cases.description: Create a Developer SKU API Management service with the .NET ARM SDK and wait for provisioning to complete.

use_cases.title: Publish APIs Through Products
use_cases.description: Create APIs, group them into products, and configure subscriptions for controlled developer access.

use_cases.title: Automate APIM Policy Updates
use_cases.description: Generate .NET code that applies XML policies for rate limits, headers, and other APIM behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Set Up Authentication
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-mgmt-apimanagement-dotnet skill to show a minimal .NET setup for DefaultAzureCredential and ArmClient. Do not print secrets.

prompt_templates.title: Create an APIM Service
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-mgmt-apimanagement-dotnet skill to write .NET guidance for creating a Developer SKU API Management service in an existing resource group.

prompt_templates.title: Configure API Products
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-mgmt-apimanagement-dotnet skill to outline how to create an API, attach it to a product, and enable subscription access.

prompt_templates.title: Design Production Automation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the azure-mgmt-apimanagement-dotnet skill to design a production APIM automation flow with policies, backup, restore, error handling, and secret redaction.

output_examples.input: Create an APIM service with the .NET management SDK.
output_examples.output: A step-by-step plan covering package installation, DefaultAzureCredential, subscription lookup, resource group access, service data, SKU selection, and long-running operation handling.

output_examples.input: Add an API to a product and require subscriptions.
output_examples.output: A concise workflow explaining API creation, product creation, product-to-API association, subscription configuration, and checks before publishing.

output_examples.input: Apply a rate limit policy safely.
output_examples.output: A policy-focused explanation that identifies the correct APIM resource scope, XML policy format, update call, and validation steps before rollout.

best_practices: Use DefaultAzureCredential with managed identity where possible, and avoid placing secrets in prompts or logs.
Use WaitUntil.Started for long provisioning operations when callers should continue other work.
Handle RequestFailedException cases explicitly and make create-or-update operations idempotent.
anti_patterns: Do not print subscription keys, client secrets, or policy secrets to console output.
Do not use this skill for APIM gateway data-plane requests or runtime API traffic.
Do not assume sample names, regions, SKUs, or quotas are valid for every Azure tenant.

faq.question: What Azure SDK package does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers Azure.ResourceManager.ApiManagement for .NET management-plane operations against Azure Resource Manager.

faq.question: Does this skill manage APIM gateway traffic?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on creating and managing APIM resources, not calling APIs through the gateway.

faq.question: Which authentication approach does it recommend?
faq.answer: It recommends DefaultAzureCredential, with managed identity preferred for deployed automation.

faq.question: Can it help create APIM policies?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for applying APIM policies with XML policy content through the management SDK.

faq.question: Is it safe to use the subscription key example as written?
faq.answer: No. Treat keys as secrets, redact output, and avoid logging retrieved subscription keys.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
